title: Geometry of 2-dimensional Riemannian disks and spheres.
speaker: Regina Rotman
affiliation: University of Toronto; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will discuss some geometric inequalities that hold on Riemannian 2-disks and 2-spheres. For example, I will prove that on any Riemannian 2-sphere there M exist at least three simple periodic geodesics of length at most 20d, where d is the diameter of M, (joint with A. Nabutovsky, Y. Liokumovich). This is a quantitative version of the well-known Lyusternik and Shnirelman theorem.

proof_stages.label: Effective closed-geodesic problem on the 2-sphere
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the Lyusternik--Shnirelman theorem giving three simple closed geodesics on every Riemannian 2-sphere and formulates effective length bounds, especially the diameter bound of at most 20 times the diameter.

proof_stages.label: Lyusternik--Shnirelman min-max cycles in the curve space
proof_stages.summary: The classical proof is recalled via relative homology classes in the space of nonparametrized simple closed curves, whose controlled representatives would give controlled simple closed geodesics by curve shortening/min-max.

proof_stages.label: Obstruction to short diameter sweepouts
proof_stages.summary: Because some Riemannian spheres or disks cannot be swept out or contracted by curves short only in terms of diameter, the proof cannot rely on an ordinary short sweepout and must seek different homologous representatives.

proof_stages.label: Homologous meridional representatives
proof_stages.summary: The standard three Lyusternik--Shnirelman cycles on the round sphere are replaced by homologous cycles built from pairs of meridians, so that bounding the images of meridians under a diffeomorphism to the given sphere would bound all min-max curves.

proof_stages.label: Croke decomposition and recursive curve shortening
proof_stages.summary: Choosing diameter-realizing points, the sphere is decomposed into convex digons and curve shortening either contracts each boundary curve to a point, producing the required meridional sweepout, or produces short geodesics directly and the process is repeated until three are obtained.

proof_stages.label: Index issue and area-diameter effective LS theorem
proof_stages.summary: The diameter-only construction may produce index-zero geodesics, so an effective Lyusternik--Shnirelman theorem with index at least one is recovered by allowing bounds depending on both area and diameter through disk-contraction and sphere-sweepout results.

proof_stages.label: From arbitrary contractions to slicings and monotone homotopies
proof_stages.summary: The second part asks whether optimal contractions or sweepouts really need self-intersections or mutual intersections, then states results converting length-controlled homotopies to simple-curve isotopies, slicings, and finally monotone homotopies without increasing length.

proof_stages.label: Discretization, switching obstruction, merging lemma, and zigzags
proof_stages.summary: A proof sketch for monotonicity discretizes the homotopy, analyzes why naive local switching of intersecting curves fails, and overcomes the discontinuity using a merging lemma and a zigzag decomposition whose optimum is monotone.

examples.name: Long ellipsoid of revolution
examples.note: A highly elongated ellipsoid of revolution has fixed/small-area behavior but a long second simple closed geodesic, showing why area alone cannot control the desired three geodesics.
examples.engagement_reason: the long ellipsoid of revolution is only invoked as a counterexample to area-only bounds, with the fact that the relevant geodesic length is long simply stated

passes.reason: a rotationally symmetric prolate ellipsoid is used to rule out an area-only bound for the second closed geodesic

A highly elongated rotational ellipsoid is used to refute area-only bounds for the second closed geodesic, and its large aspect ratio/axial symmetry make it extremal and symmetric rather than generic.
A prolate ellipsoid of revolution is rotationally symmetric and taken in an elongated limiting regime to refute an area-only bound.
Prolate spheroids form a rotationally symmetric high-aspect-ratio limiting family used to refute an area-only geodesic bound.
A rotationally symmetric, highly elongated ellipsoid is used to refute an area-only bound for the second closed geodesic.
A highly elongated surface of revolution is used to rule out an area-only bound on the relevant periodic-geodesic lengths.
A highly elongated axisymmetric ellipsoid gives an unbounded area-normalized second geodesic length, obstructing any area-only bound.
used to refute an area-only bound, and the highly elongated ellipsoid is a limiting high-aspect-ratio sphere metric
Used to rule out area-only length bounds; the prolate surface of revolution is a highly elongated limiting family.
Used to rule out an area-only bound, and the prolate surface of revolution has rotational symmetry with extreme aspect ratio.
rotationally symmetric, highly elongated spheroids refute area-only bounds for the second closed geodesic
An elongated rotational ellipsoid is a symmetric high-aspect-ratio limiting family used to refute an area-only bound for multiple closed geodesics.

examples.name: Metrics with necessarily long sweepouts
examples.note: The Liokumovich/Nabutovsky construction, based on Frankel--Katz counterexamples to Gromov's disk-contraction question, shows that diameter-only short sweepouts cannot be expected and motivates replacing sweepouts by homologous meridional representatives.
examples.engagement_reason: the family of metrics with necessarily long sweepouts is cited as an existing result, but no construction or calculation is run on those metrics

passes.reason: the constructed metrics are used to show that diameter alone cannot bound sweepout lengths
These constructed metrics have arbitrarily large required sweepout length relative to diameter, serving as counterexamples to short-sweepout expectations.
These metrics have sweepout width arbitrarily large relative to diameter, serving as counterexamples to diameter-only short sweepouts.
The constructed metrics have arbitrarily large sweepout width relative to diameter and serve as the negative answer to short diameter-controlled sweepouts.
These constructed metrics have arbitrarily large sweepout width relative to diameter, giving the negative answer to short-sweepout bounds.
These metrics have sweepout width arbitrarily large relative to diameter, giving the negative answer to the hoped-for short-sweepout bound.
The constructed metrics have unusually large sweepout width and are cited as negative answers to diameter-only sweepout bounds.
constructed to show diameter-only short sweepouts cannot always exist, with sweepout width made intrinsically large
Used as a negative answer to short sweepouts in terms of diameter alone; their minimax sweepout width is made arbitrarily large.
The constructed metrics show diameter-only short sweepouts can fail, with sweepout width forced to be unusually large.
metrics with large intrinsic sweepout width are used to disprove diameter-only short sweepout bounds
These constructed metrics have sweepout width arbitrarily large relative to diameter, giving a negative answer to diameter-only short-sweepout bounds.

examples.name: Hills and valleys causing tangled contractions
examples.note: The imagined Riemannian disk with topographic obstacles illustrates why an apparently optimal contraction of the boundary might be suspected to develop self-intersections or intersect earlier curves.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker sketches a disk with hills or valleys and reasons through how an attempted contraction may develop self-intersections or intersections with other curves

passes.reason: the hills-and-valleys picture is only a motivating possible pathology, not an actual refutation of monotone contraction
The hills-and-valleys disk is a motivating imagined obstruction to nice contractions, not an actual deployed counterexample, and has no special classified structure.
The hills-and-valleys disk is a schematic ordinary Riemannian disk motivating possible tangling rather than proving a claim false.
A hilly or valleyed disk is just a generic motivating picture of possible tangled contractions, not an established disproof.
An otherwise ordinary bumpy disk is invoked as a possible enemy case where an optimal contraction might tangle.
The hills-and-valleys disk picture is a motivating possible pathology for contractions, not an actual example proving a claim false.
A topographically complicated disk is evoked as an adversarial scenario for monotone contractions, with no special intrinsic structure specified.
a generic bumpy disk scenario is only a motivating potential pathology, not an actual disproof of the monotone-contraction result

examples.name: Nonunique switching at tangential intersections
examples.note: The configuration of two discretized curves meeting tangentially shows why the naive shortest-piece switching procedure can be discontinuous, leading to the need for the merging lemma and zigzag organization.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker draws intersecting curves, tries the switching operation on pieces, and analyzes the tangential case where nonunique switching causes discontinuity

passes.reason: the tangential nontransverse intersection is a degenerate configuration obstructing the naive switching construction
A tangential intersection is a nontransverse degenerate boundary case, used to show the naive switching procedure can fail by nonuniqueness/discontinuity.
A tangential intersection is a nontransverse degenerate curve configuration used to show the naive switching procedure is not well-defined.
Tangential curve contact is a nontransverse boundary case that obstructs the naive switching argument by making switching nonunique.
